verses.commentary: Creation is complete. The heavens and the earth and all their vast array are finished. The Hebrew word (tsaba, צְבָא) can mean host or army, suggesting an organized array in formation. Creation is not chaotic but ordered, purposeful, and complete.

God rested (shabat, שָׁבַת) on the seventh day. This is not rest from exhaustion but cessation from labor, completion. God's rest establishes a pattern for human life. Work has an endpoint. Productivity is not ultimate. Rest is built into the fabric of creation itself.

God blessed and made holy (qadash, קָדַשׁ) the seventh day. To make holy means to set apart, to mark as different. Time itself can be sacred. The Sabbath is the first thing in creation declared holy, even before the temple or priesthood. Holiness begins with time, not space.

This begins a second creation account with a different perspective. Genesis 1 gave the cosmic view; Genesis 2 zooms in on humanity and relationships. The name shifts to LORD God (YHWH Elohim), the personal covenant name combined with the generic term for God.

Before vegetation, before rain, there was no one to work the ground. Humanity is needed for creation to flourish. We are not afterthoughts but essential participants in God's created order. Creation awaits human cultivation and care.

Streams watered the whole surface of the ground. The earth has its own life-giving systems even before human involvement. God provides the conditions for life. Water flows from the earth itself, sustaining creation.

The LORD God formed (yatsar, יָצַר) man from dust of the ground (adamah, אֲדָמָה). The verb suggests a potter shaping clay, intimate and hands-on. Humanity comes from the earth, is named after the earth (adam from adamah), and will return to it. We are earthlings, grounded. God breathed into his nostrils the breath of life (nishmat chayim, נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים), and man became a living being. We are both earth and spirit, material and divine breath combined.

God planted a garden in Eden, in the east. Eden (עֵדֶן) may mean delight or pleasure. This is not wild nature but cultivated space, a partnership between divine planting and human dwelling. The garden is a temple-like space where God and humanity meet.

God made trees pleasing to the eye and good for food. Beauty and utility together. The aesthetic and the practical are both gifts from God. The tree of life and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil stand in the middle. These are not magical but symbolic, representing life itself and moral autonomy.
A river watering the garden flowed from Eden. Water is the source of life, flowing outward from this sacred center. The garden is abundantly provided for, a place of plenty.
The first river, Pishon, winds through Havilah where there is gold. The text locates Eden in real geography, not pure myth. The abundance of resources suggests creation's generosity.
The gold of that land is good; aromatic resin and onyx are also there. These are materials later used in the tabernacle. Eden prefigures the temple, a place where heaven and earth meet.
The second river, Gihon, winds through Cush. The geographical details anchor the story in the real world while pointing to a primordial time before current geography.

The third river, Tigris, runs along the east side of Ashur. The fourth river is the Euphrates. These are real rivers in Mesopotamia, the cradle of civilization. Eden is both historical memory and theological ideal.

The LORD God took the man and put him in the Garden of Eden to work it (abad, עָבַד) and take care of it (shamar, שָׁמַר). The same words used for priestly service in the temple. Work is not a curse but a calling. To work and keep, to serve and guard, this is humanity's vocation. We are gardener-priests.

God commanded the man: You are free to eat from any tree in the garden. Notice the abundance first. Permission precedes prohibition. Freedom is the context. God's first words to humanity are generous.

But you must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, for when you eat from it you will certainly die. One limit in boundless freedom. The knowledge of good and evil may mean moral autonomy, the power to define right and wrong apart from God. Death enters not as punishment but as consequence. To grasp autonomy is to sever the source of life.

The LORD God said, It is not good for the man to be alone. The first thing declared not good in creation. Humanity is made for relationship. Solitude is incomplete. I will make a helper (ezer, עֵזֶר) suitable for him. The word ezer is used of God helping Israel, a strong term meaning ally, partner, not subordinate.

God formed out of the ground all the wild animals and birds and brought them to the man to see what he would name them. Naming is an act of authority and creativity. God invites the man to participate in ordering creation. Whatever the man called each living creature, that was its name. Human language and culture are affirmed.

The man gave names to all livestock, birds, and wild animals. But for Adam no suitable helper was found. The animals are companions but not equals. Naming them reveals the man's solitude. None correspond to him. He is still alone.

The LORD God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep (tardemah, תַּרְדֵּמָה). This deep sleep appears elsewhere at moments of divine revelation. The man is passive; God is the creator. God took one of the man's ribs and closed up the place with flesh. The word often translated rib (tsela, צֵלָע) can mean side. Woman is not an afterthought or addition but drawn from humanity itself.

The LORD God made (banah, בָּנָה) a woman from the rib (side). The verb means built, the same word used for constructing buildings. This suggests care, architecture, intentional design. God brought her to the man. Like a father presenting the bride.

The man said, This is now bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh. The first poetry in Scripture, a love song. She shall be called woman (ishshah, אִשָּׁה) for she was taken out of man (ish, אִישׁ). The wordplay in Hebrew shows their correspondence. They are counterparts, one flesh divided and reunited.

That is why a man leaves his father and mother and is united to his wife, and they become one flesh. Marriage is a return to original unity. Two becoming one reflects the pre-separation state. The leaving and cleaving establishes a new primary loyalty. Family is redefined around the marriage bond.

Adam and his wife were both naked and felt no shame. Innocence, vulnerability, openness. Shame is not yet present because sin has not entered. Nakedness is natural, unguarded intimacy. This is the ideal before the fall: transparency, trust, no hiding.
